excerpt: The Imam is either settled (mustaqarr) in his own person or entrusted (mustawdaʿ) for a time, that the lamp of guidance not be extinguished between two settled lights.
source_location_hint: Discussion of the ranks of the Imamate (mustaqarr / mustawdaʿ) and their place in the cosmic hierarchy

excerpt: The light of Muḥammad and the light of ʿAlī are one light, divided in appearance and united in essence, that the bearers of the daʿwa might know the secret of the two who are one.
source_authority: Chief dāʿī of the Fatimid daʿwa (d. 470/1078)
source_location_hint: Sermons on the unity of the prophetic and walāya lights and the doctrine of paired imamate

excerpt: The letters are of two kinds: those of light, which signify the ranks of the spiritual hierarchy, and those of darkness, which signify what is veiled and composite in the world of bodies.
source_location_hint: Discussion in the early yanābīʿ of letter-symbolism in taʾwīl

excerpt: The First Intellect (al-ʿAql al-Awwal) is the alif of being: a single, undivided origination from the Originator, Most High (al-Mubdiʿ Taʿālā), upon which the order of all subsequent letters and ranks depends.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the First Intellect as the source of intelligible being and the symbol of the alif

excerpt: The luminous and corporeal intellects (al-ʿuqūl al-nūrāniyya wa-l-jismāniyya) constitute the heavenly hierarchy whose terrestrial counterpart is the daʿwa.
source_location_hint: sections on the hierarchy of intellects (yanābīʿ on al-ʿaql)

excerpt: The nāṭiq receives continual mumādda (sustaining support) from the higher ḥudūd, which preserves his utterance from error.
source_location_hint: discussions of the prophetic faculty and the influx from the higher hierarchy

excerpt: The Imamate alternates between mustaqarr (settled) and mustawdaʿ (entrusted) custodians, a doctrine elaborated in early Ismaili kashf literature.
source_location_hint: sections on the categories of Imāms (mustaqarr and mustawdaʿ)

excerpt: The angelic reinforcement at Badr is read esoterically as the gathering of supporting hierarchies from prior prophetic cycles.
source_location_hint: taʾwīl of jihād and the assistance of angels

excerpt: The pairing of ʿAyn and Mīm functions as a cipher for ʿAlī and Muḥammad, whose inseparability is affirmed across every dispensation.
source_location_hint: majālis on the walāya of ʿAlī and the unity of Muḥammad-ʿAlī

excerpt: The lower ranks (surrāt) become meaningful only when integrated into the haykal of the Imām, who confers ontological meaning upon the otherwise meaningless letters of the ḥudūd.
source_location_hint: sections on the relation between the ḥudūd ʿulwiyya and the corporeal world

excerpt: Nāṣir-i Khusraw treats the disjoined letters (muqaṭṭaʿāt) as ciphers of the cosmic hierarchy of nuṭaqāʾ and their hudūd, arguing that each letter-group encodes the rank-relations between the Speaker-Prophets and the spiritual limits supporting them.
source_location_hint: On the taʾwīl of the muqaṭṭaʿāt and the seven nuṭaqāʾ

excerpt: Al-Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān composed Daʿāʾim al-Islām under the patronage of al-Muʿizz li-Dīn Allāh, codifying Fatimid Ismaili jurisprudence as the official madhhab of the dynasty.
source_authority: Chief dāʿī and judge under the Fatimid caliphs al-Manṣūr and al-Muʿizz (d. 363/974)

excerpt: Al-Muʾayyad fī al-Dīn al-Shīrāzī expounds in his Majālis on the convergence of the lights of the Ahl al-Kisāʾ (the Five of the Cloak) in the person of the Prophet, and their transmission to ʿAlī and the Imams of his progeny as the substrate of the Imamate.
source_authority: Bāb al-abwāb and chief dāʿī under the Fatimid caliph al-Mustanṣir (d. 470/1078)
source_location_hint: Majālis on the Ahl al-Kisāʾ and the lights of the Imamate

excerpt: Jaʿfar ibn Manṣūr al-Yaman devotes Asrār al-Nuṭaqāʾ to the esoteric meanings of the seven Speaker-Prophets, including the symbolic significance of their names and the cosmic ranks (ḥudūd) corresponding to each.
source_location_hint: On the names of the seven nuṭaqāʾ and their hudūd

excerpt: Ḥamīd al-Dīn al-Kirmānī systematizes the doctrine of the ten intellects (al-ʿuqūl al-ʿashara) as the upper hudūd (ḥudūd ʿulwiyya) of the cosmos, each sustaining the rank of a Speaker-Prophet and his successors below.
source_authority: Ḥujjat of the Iraqi daʿwa under the Fatimid caliph al-Ḥākim (d. ca. 411/1020)
source_location_hint: On the ten intellects as the spiritual hierarchy supporting the nuṭaqāʾ

excerpt: Walāya for ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib and the imams of his progeny is held by the daʿwa as the seventh pillar of Islam and the inward seal of the prophetic message.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya (opening book of the Daʿāʾim)
